changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Pleasant Weather"

TextENGNew: The one thing I could boast about was the ventilation.
changeReason: Old adds 'I like my house' (absent from JPN) and uses present tense; JPN '自慢だった' is past tense and simply states the ventilation was the one thing to boast about.

TextENGNew: As long as a breeze was blowing, I could stay perfectly cool without any air conditioning.
changeReason: Old translates '冷房' (air conditioning) as 'fan'; new correctly renders it as 'air conditioning.'

TextENGNew: I pulled in the futon I had been airing in the wide-open window, laid out a sitting cushion in its place, and settled in to enjoy the cool of the evening from the veranda.
changeReason: Old uses present tense while JPN and new use past tense; old says 'near the open window' while new correctly identifies '縁側' as 'veranda,' a distinct location.

TextENGNew: The page-a-day calendar told me it was June, Showa 58 -- 1983.
changeReason: New preserves 'Showa 58' from the JPN '昭和の５８年,' retaining the Japanese era dating that the old translation omits entirely.

TextENGNew: Despite it being only June, the rainy season had failed to materialize this year, and summer already seemed to be in full swing.@k...Apparently this was what they call abnormal weather.
changeReason: Old says summer was 'coming around the corner' (approaching) while JPN '夏本番の到来' and new say summer was already 'in full swing' (arrived), a different stage of the season.

TextENGNew: Even if this was a once-in-a-century anomaly, if it was destined to occur in the year 1983, there was nothing remarkable about it.
It was simply inevitable.
Far more unpredictable and extraordinary was a sudden afternoon cloudburst catching you on the walk home from school.
Every single day proceeded in perfect, preordained harmony.
And yet, somehow, I had a feeling that this year was off to an auspicious start.
changeReason: Old says 'something good was going to happen' (vague future prediction), while JPN '幸先がいい' and new say 'off to an auspicious start' (present observation about omens), a different temporal framing.

TextENGNew: ...How should I put it?
It was like the feeling you get when you roll a six on the very first throw of a board game and start off with a commanding lead.
Of course, the more you roll a die, the more the total converges toward the average.
In our daily lives, we are constantly rolling dice in all manner of ways.
So there is no reason to get carried away over one small stroke of luck.
...If the first roll is a six and the next is a one, that makes seven -- dead average.
A fatalist might claim the next roll has a higher probability of being a one.
But the truth is, the next roll could be anything from one to six.@kThat is the nature of fate.
...Perhaps the next roll will be a six as well.
Taken together, two consecutive sixes would be a one-in-thirty-six miracle, but... if you take it one roll at a time, it is merely a one-in-six chance.
The glass wind chime Satoko had hung up yesterday was making such a refreshingly cool sound...
